title: TinyNav: End-to-End TinyML for Real-Time Autonomous Navigation on Microcontrollers
comment: 6 pages, 7 figures, presented at CUCAI2026 (Canadian Undergraduate Conference on AI, https://cucai.ca)
summary: Autonomous navigation typically relies on power-intensive processors, limiting accessibility in low-cost robotics. Although microcontrollers offer a resource-efficient alternative, they impose strict constraints on model complexity. We present TinyNav, an end-to-end TinyML system for real-time autonomous navigation on an ESP32 microcontroller. A custom-trained, quantized 2D convolutional neural network processes a 20-frame sliding window of depth data to predict steering and throttle commands. By avoiding 3D convolutions and recurrent layers, the 23k-parameter model achieves 30 ms inference latency. Correlation analysis and Grad-CAM validation indicate consistent spatial awareness and obstacle avoidance behavior. TinyNav demonstrates that responsive autonomous control can be deployed directly on highly constrained edge devices, reducing reliance on external compute resources.

AI.tldr: TinyNav is an end-to-end TinyML system for real-time autonomous navigation on ESP32 microcontrollers, using a quantized 2D CNN (23k parameters) with 30 ms inference latency to process depth data for steering and throttle control.
AI.motivation: Autonomous navigation typically relies on power-intensive processors, limiting accessibility in low-cost robotics. Microcontrollers offer resource-efficient alternatives but impose strict constraints on model complexity.
AI.method: TinyNav uses a custom-trained, quantized 2D convolutional neural network (23k parameters) that processes a 20-frame sliding window of depth data to predict steering and throttle commands. The system avoids 3D convolutions and recurrent layers to maintain efficiency on the ESP32 microcontroller.
AI.result: The model achieves 30 ms inference latency and correlation analysis combined with Grad-CAM validation demonstrates consistent spatial awareness and obstacle avoidance behavior.
AI.conclusion: TinyNav demonstrates that responsive autonomous control can be deployed directly on highly constrained edge devices, reducing reliance on external compute resources.

title: OA-NBV: Occlusion-Aware Next-Best-View Planning for Human-Centered Active Perception on Mobile Robots
summary: We naturally step sideways or lean to see around the obstacle when our view is blocked, and recover a more informative observation. Enabling robots to make the same kind of viewpoint choice is critical for human-centered operations, including search, triage, and disaster response, where cluttered environments and partial visibility frequently degrade downstream perception. However, many Next-Best-View (NBV) methods primarily optimize generic exploration or long-horizon coverage, and do not explicitly target the immediate goal of obtaining a single usable observation of a partially occluded person under real motion constraints. We present Occlusion-Aware Next-Best-View Planning for Human-Centered Active Perception on Mobile Robots (OA-NBV), an occlusion-aware NBV pipeline that autonomously selects the next traversable viewpoint to obtain a more complete view of an occluded human. OA-NBV integrates perception and motion planning by scoring candidate viewpoints using a target-centric visibility model that accounts for occlusion, target scale, and target completeness, while restricting candidates to feasible robot poses. OA-NBV achieves over 90% success rate in both simulation and real-world trials, while baseline NBV methods degrade sharply under occlusion. Beyond success rate, OA-NBV improves observation quality: compared to the strongest baseline, it increases normalized target area by at least 81% and keypoint visibility by at least 58% across settings, making it a drop-in view-selection module for diverse human-centered downstream tasks.

AI.tldr: OA-NBV is an occlusion-aware Next-Best-View planning method that enables mobile robots to autonomously select optimal viewpoints to obtain complete views of occluded humans by integrating perception and motion planning, achieving over 90% success rate and significantly improving observation quality.
AI.motivation: Humans naturally adjust their posture to see around obstacles when their view is blocked. In human-centered robotic tasks (search, triage, disaster response), cluttered environments and partial visibility frequently degrade perception. Existing Next-Best-View methods primarily optimize generic exploration or long-horizon coverage, failing to explicitly target the immediate goal of obtaining a single usable observation of an occluded human under real motion constraints.
AI.method: OA-NBV integrates perception and motion planning by scoring candidate viewpoints using a target-centric visibility model that accounts for occlusion, target scale, and target completeness. The method restricts candidates to feasible robot poses, creating an occlusion-aware pipeline that autonomously selects the next traversable viewpoint to obtain a more complete view of an occluded human.
AI.result: OA-NBV achieves over 90% success rate in both simulation and real-world trials, while baseline NBV methods degrade sharply under occlusion. Compared to the strongest baseline, OA-NBV increases normalized target area by at least 81% and keypoint visibility by at least 58% across different settings.
AI.conclusion: OA-NBV is a drop-in view-selection module for diverse human-centered downstream tasks, demonstrating high success rates and significantly improved observation quality through its occlusion-aware approach to Next-Best-View planning.

title: DRAFTO: Decoupled Reduced-space and Adaptive Feasibility-repair Trajectory Optimization for Robotic Manipulators
summary: This paper introduces a new algorithm for trajectory optimization, Decoupled Reduced-space and Adaptive Feasibility-repair Trajectory Optimization (DRAFTO). It first constructs a constrained objective that accounts for smoothness, safety, joint limits, and task requirements. Then, it optimizes the coefficients, which are the coordinates of a set of basis functions for trajectory parameterization. To reduce the number of repeated constrained optimizations while handling joint-limit feasibility, the optimization is decoupled into a reduced-space Gauss-Newton (GN) descent for the main iterations and constrained quadratic programming for initialization and terminal feasibility repair. The two-phase acceptance rule with a non-monotone policy is applied to the GN model, which uses a hinge-squared penalty for inequality constraints, to ensure globalizability. The results of our benchmark tests against optimization-based planners, such as CHOMP, TrajOpt, GPMP2, and FACTO, and sampling-based planners, such as RRT-Connect, RRT*, and PRM, validate the high efficiency and reliability across diverse scenarios and tasks. The experiment involving grabbing an object from a drawer further demonstrates the potential for implementation in complex manipulation tasks. The supplemental video is available at https://youtu.be/XisFI37YyTQ.

AI.tldr: DRAFTO is a new trajectory optimization algorithm that decouples optimization into reduced-space Gauss-Newton descent for main iterations and constrained quadratic programming for feasibility repair, using a hinge-squared penalty and non-monotone policy for globalizability.
AI.motivation: To develop an efficient and reliable trajectory optimization algorithm that handles constraints (smoothness, safety, joint limits, task requirements) while reducing repeated constrained optimizations and ensuring globalizability.
AI.method: 1) Constructs a constrained objective with smoothness, safety, joint limits, and task requirements; 2) Optimizes coefficients of basis functions for trajectory parameterization; 3) Decouples optimization into reduced-space Gauss-Newton descent (main iterations) and constrained quadratic programming (initialization and terminal feasibility repair); 4) Applies two-phase acceptance rule with non-monotone policy using hinge-squared penalty for inequality constraints.
AI.result: Benchmark tests against CHOMP, TrajOpt, GPMP2, FACTO (optimization-based) and RRT-Connect, RRT*, PRM (sampling-based) show high efficiency and reliability across diverse scenarios and tasks, including complex manipulation tasks like grabbing an object from a drawer.
AI.conclusion: DRAFTO demonstrates high efficiency and reliability for trajectory optimization in complex manipulation tasks, showing strong potential for real-world implementation.

title: TATIC: Task-Aware Temporal Learning for Human Intent Inference from Physical Corrections in Human-Robot Collaboration
summary: In human-robot collaboration (HRC), robots must adapt online to dynamic task constraints and evolving human intent. While physical corrections provide a natural, low-latency channel for operators to convey motion-level adjustments, extracting task-level semantic intent from such brief interactions remains challenging. Existing foundation-model-based approaches primarily rely on vision and language inputs and lack mechanisms to interpret physical feedback. Meanwhile, traditional physical human-robot interaction (pHRI) methods leverage physical corrections for trajectory guidance but struggle to infer task-level semantics. To bridge this gap, we propose TATIC, a unified framework that utilizes torque-based contact force estimation and a task-aware Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) to jointly infer discrete task-level intent and estimate continuous motion-level parameters from brief physical corrections. Task-aligned feature canonicalization ensures robust generalization across diverse layouts, while an intent-driven adaptation scheme translates inferred human intent into robot motion adaptations. Experiments achieve a 0.904 Macro-F1 score in intent recognition and demonstrate successful hardware validation in collaborative disassembly (see experimental video at https://youtu.be/xF8A52qwEc8).

AI.tldr: TATIC is a human-robot collaboration framework that uses torque-based contact force estimation and a task-aware Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) to jointly infer discrete task-level intent and continuous motion parameters from brief physical corrections, achieving 0.904 Macro-F1 score in intent recognition and successful hardware validation in collaborative disassembly.
AI.motivation: Robots in HRC must adapt to dynamic task constraints and evolving human intent. Physical corrections provide a natural, low-latency channel for operators to convey motion-level adjustments, but extracting task-level semantic intent from these brief interactions remains challenging. Existing foundation-model approaches rely primarily on vision and language inputs without physical feedback mechanisms, while traditional pHRI methods leverage physical corrections for trajectory guidance but lack task-level semantic inference capabilities.
AI.method: TATIC utilizes torque-based contact force estimation combined with a task-aware Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) to jointly infer discrete task-level intent and estimate continuous motion-level parameters from brief physical corrections. The framework includes task-aligned feature canonicalization for robust generalization across diverse layouts, and an intent-driven adaptation scheme that translates inferred human intent into robot motion adaptations.
AI.result: Experiments achieve a 0.904 Macro-F1 score in intent recognition and demonstrate successful hardware validation in collaborative disassembly tasks, with experimental video available at https://youtu.be/xF8A52qwEc8.
AI.conclusion: TATIC successfully bridges the gap between physical interaction and semantic intent understanding in human-robot collaboration by leveraging torque-based force estimation and TCN-based analysis to jointly infer discrete intent and continuous motion parameters from brief physical corrections.

title: Enhancing Value Alignment of LLMs with Multi-agent system and Combinatorial Fusion
comment: 5 pages, 3 figures, accepted to 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech, and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
summary: Aligning large language models (LLMs) with human values is a central challenge for ensuring trustworthy and safe deployment. While existing methods such as Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) and its variants have improved alignment, they often rely on a single evaluator or narrowly defined reward signals, limiting their ability to capture ethical pluralism. In this work, we propose the Value Alignment System using Combinatorial Fusion Analysis (VAS-CFA), a framework that operationalizes multi-agent fusion alignment. It instantiates multiple moral agents, each fine-tuned to represent a distinct normative perspective, and fuses their outputs using CFA with both rank- and score-based aggregation. This design leverages cognitive diversity, between agents, to mitigate conflicts and redundancies across multiple agents, producing responses that better reflect human values. Empirical evaluation demonstrates that VAS-CFA outperforms both single agent baselines and prior aggregation approaches on standard metrics, showing that multi-agent fusion provides a robust and effective mechanism for advancing value alignment in LLMs.

AI.tldr: VAS-CFA is a multi-agent fusion alignment framework that combines multiple fine-tuned moral agents using Combinatorial Fusion Analysis to better align LLMs with diverse human values.
AI.motivation: Existing alignment methods like RLHF rely on single evaluators or narrow reward signals, limiting their ability to capture ethical pluralism and diverse human values.
AI.method: VAS-CFA instantiates multiple moral agents, each fine-tuned to represent a distinct normative perspective, and fuses their outputs using Combinatorial Fusion Analysis (CFA) with both rank- and score-based aggregation to leverage cognitive diversity.
AI.result: VAS-CFA outperforms both single agent baselines and prior aggregation approaches on standard metrics, demonstrating that multi-agent fusion provides a robust mechanism for advancing value alignment.
AI.conclusion: Multi-agent fusion, implemented through VAS-CFA, effectively improves value alignment in LLMs by mitigating conflicts and redundancies across agents and producing responses that better reflect human values.

title: SELF-VLA: A Skill Enhanced Agentic Vision-Language-Action Framework for Contact-Rich Disassembly
summary: Disassembly automation has long been pursued to address the growing demand for efficient and proper recovery of valuable components from the end-of-life (EoL) electronic products. Existing approaches have demonstrated promising and regimented performance by decomposing the disassembly process into different subtasks. However, each subtask typically requires extensive data preparation, model training, and system management. Moreover, these approaches are often task- and component-specific, making them poorly suited to handle the variability and uncertainty of EoL products and limiting their generalization capabilities. All these factors restrict the practical deployment of current robotic disassembly systems and leave them highly reliant on human labor. With the recent development of foundation models in robotics, vision-language-action (VLA) models have shown impressive performance on standard robotic manipulation tasks, but their applicability to complex, contact-rich, and long-horizon industrial practices like disassembly, which requires sequential and precise manipulation, remains limited. To address this challenge, we propose SELF-VLA, an agentic VLA framework that integrates explicit disassembly skills. Experimental studies demonstrate that our framework significantly outperforms current state-of-the-art end-to-end VLA models on two contact-rich disassembly tasks. The video illustration can be found via https://zh.engr.tamu.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/310/2026/03/IROS-VLA-Video.mp4.

AI.tldr: SELF-VLA is an agentic vision-language-action framework that integrates explicit disassembly skills to overcome limitations of existing robotic disassembly systems, achieving superior performance on contact-rich disassembly tasks compared to end-to-end VLA models.
AI.motivation: Disassembly automation is essential for recovering valuable components from end-of-life electronics, but existing approaches are task/component-specific, require extensive preparation, and lack generalization. While VLA models excel at standard manipulation tasks, they struggle with complex, contact-rich, long-horizon industrial disassembly requiring sequential precise manipulation.
AI.method: The researchers proposed SELF-VLA, an agentic VLA framework that integrates explicit disassembly skills, building upon foundation models in robotics. The framework was tested on two contact-rich disassembly tasks, comparing its performance against current state-of-the-art end-to-end VLA models.
AI.result: Experimental studies demonstrate that SELF-VLA significantly outperforms current state-of-the-art end-to-end VLA models on two contact-rich disassembly tasks.
AI.conclusion: By integrating explicit disassembly skills into the VLA framework, SELF-VLA addresses the limitations of existing approaches and achieves superior performance in complex industrial disassembly scenarios, demonstrating better generalization capabilities for handling the variability and uncertainty of end-of-life products.

title: How Intelligence Emerges: A Minimal Theory of Dynamic Adaptive Coordination
summary:
This paper develops a dynamical theory of adaptive coordination in multi-agent systems. Rather than analyzing coordination through equilibrium optimization or agent-centric learning alone, the framework models agents, incentives, and environment as a recursively closed feedback architecture. A persistent environment stores accumulated coordination signals, a distributed incentive field transmits those signals locally, and adaptive agents update in response. Coordination is thus treated as a structural property of coupled dynamics rather than as the solution to a centralized objective.
  The paper establishes three structural results. First, under dissipativity assumptions, the induced closed-loop system admits a bounded forward-invariant region, ensuring viability without requiring global optimality. Second, when incentive signals depend non-trivially on persistent environmental memory, the resulting dynamics generically cannot be reduced to a static global objective defined solely over the agent state space. Third, persistent environmental state induces history sensitivity unless the system is globally contracting.
  A minimal linear specification illustrates how coupling, persistence, and dissipation govern local stability and oscillatory regimes through spectral conditions on the Jacobian. The results establish structural conditions under which intelligent coordination dynamics emerge from incentive-mediated adaptive interaction within a persistent environment, without presuming welfare maximization, rational expectations, or centralized design.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a dynamical theory of adaptive coordination in multi-agent systems by modeling agents, incentives, and environment as a recursively closed feedback architecture, establishing structural conditions for coordination without requiring equilibrium optimization or centralized design.
AI.motivation: Rather than analyzing coordination through equilibrium optimization or agent-centric learning alone, the framework models agents, incentives, and environment as a recursively closed feedback architecture, treating coordination as a structural property of coupled dynamics rather than a solution to a centralized objective.
AI.method: The framework includes a persistent environment that stores accumulated coordination signals, a distributed incentive field that transmits signals locally, and adaptive agents that update in response. Three structural results are established: (1) bounded forward-invariant region under dissipativity assumptions, (2) generically irreducible dynamics when incentive signals depend on environmental memory, and (3) history sensitivity induced by persistent environmental state unless globally contracting. A minimal linear specification demonstrates how coupling, persistence, and dissipation govern stability through spectral conditions on the Jacobian.
AI.result: The paper establishes three structural results: (1) under dissipativity assumptions, the closed-loop system admits a bounded forward-invariant region ensuring viability without global optimality; (2) when incentive signals depend non-trivially on persistent environmental memory, the dynamics generically cannot be reduced to a static global objective defined solely over agent state space; (3) persistent environmental state induces history sensitivity unless the system is globally contracting.
AI.conclusion: The results establish structural conditions under which intelligent coordination dynamics emerge from incentive-mediated adaptive interaction within a persistent environment, without presuming welfare maximization, rational expectations, or centralized design. This provides a foundational theory for understanding how coordination can arise from local adaptive interactions in structured feedback systems.

title: Edge-Assisted Multi-Robot Visual-Inertial SLAM with Efficient Communication
comment: 13 pages, 18 figures
summary: The integration of cloud computing and edge computing is an effective way to achieve global consistent and real-time multi-robot Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM). Cloud computing effectively solves the problem of limited computing, communication and storage capacity of terminal equipment. However, limited bandwidth and extremely long communication links between terminal devices and the cloud result in serious performance degradation of multi-robot SLAM systems. To reduce the computational cost of feature tracking and improve the real-time performance of the robot, a lightweight SLAM method of optical flow tracking based on pyramid IMU prediction is proposed. On this basis, a centralized multi-robot SLAM system based on a robot-edge-cloud layered architecture is proposed to realize real-time collaborative SLAM. It avoids the problems of limited on-board computing resources and low execution efficiency of single robot. In this framework, only the feature points and keyframe descriptors are transmitted and lossless encoding and compression are carried out to realize real-time remote information transmission with limited bandwidth resources. This design reduces the actual bandwidth occupied in the process of data transmission, and does not cause the loss of SLAM accuracy caused by data compression. Through experimental verification on the EuRoC dataset, compared with the current most advanced local feature compression method, our method can achieve lower data volume feature transmission, and compared with the current advanced centralized multi-robot SLAM scheme, it can achieve the same or better positioning accuracy under low computational load.

AI.tldr: A lightweight optical flow SLAM method with pyramid IMU prediction is proposed, combined with a centralized multi-robot SLAM system based on robot-edge-cloud architecture that transmits only feature points and keyframe descriptors with lossless encoding to achieve real-time collaborative SLAM with reduced bandwidth usage while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: Cloud-edge computing integration enables global consistent and real-time multi-robot SLAM, but limited bandwidth and long communication links cause performance degradation. Traditional single-robot SLAM suffers from limited on-board computing resources and low execution efficiency.
AI.method: 1) Proposes a lightweight SLAM method of optical flow tracking based on pyramid IMU prediction to reduce computational cost of feature tracking. 2) Establishes a centralized multi-robot SLAM system based on robot-edge-cloud layered architecture. 3) Transmits only feature points and keyframe descriptors with lossless encoding and compression.
AI.result: On EuRoC dataset: achieves lower data volume feature transmission compared with current advanced local feature compression methods; achieves same or better positioning accuracy compared with current advanced centralized multi-robot SLAM schemes under low computational load.
AI.conclusion: The proposed design reduces actual bandwidth occupied during data transmission without causing SLAM accuracy loss, achieving real-time collaborative SLAM with reduced computational load and improved efficiency.

title: Hybrid Human-Agent Social Dilemmas in Energy Markets
comment: 20 pages, 7 figures. Submitted to Proceedings of the Royal Society A, Special Issue on "The evolution of sociality in hybrid human AI populations"
summary: In hybrid populations where humans delegate strategic decision-making to autonomous agents, understanding when and how cooperative behaviors can emerge remains a key challenge. We study this problem in the context of energy load management: consumer agents schedule their appliance use under demand-dependent pricing. This structure can create a social dilemma where everybody would benefit from coordination, but in equilibrium agents often choose to incur the congestion costs that cooperative turn-taking would avoid. To address the problem of coordination, we introduce artificial agents that use globally observable signals to increase coordination. Using evolutionary dynamics, and reinforcement learning experiments, we show that artificial agents can shift the learning dynamics to favour coordination outcomes. An often neglected problem is partial adoption: what happens when the technology of artificial agents is in the early adoption stages? We analyze mixed populations of adopters and non-adopters, demonstrating that unilateral entry is feasible: adopters are not structurally penalized, and partial adoption can still improve aggregate outcomes. However, in some parameter regimes, non-adopters may benefit disproportionately from the cooperation induced by adopters. This asymmetry, while not precluding beneficial entry, warrants consideration in deployment, and highlights strategic issues around the adoption of AI technology in multiagent settings.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how artificial agents can improve coordination in energy load management, a classic social dilemma where agents choose suboptimal strategies. The research demonstrates that artificial agents using globally observable signals can shift learning dynamics toward cooperative outcomes, and even partial adoption is beneficial. However, the study reveals an asymmetry where non-adopters can disproportionately benefit from the cooperation induced by adopters, highlighting strategic considerations for AI deployment in multiagent settings.
AI.motivation: In hybrid populations where humans delegate strategic decisions to autonomous agents (e.g., energy load management with demand-dependent pricing), understanding when and how cooperative behaviors emerge is challenging. The pricing structure creates a social dilemma: coordination would benefit everyone, but in equilibrium agents choose strategies that incur congestion costs. The motivation is to explore how artificial agents can facilitate coordination and address partial adoption challenges.
AI.method: The researchers introduce artificial agents that utilize globally observable signals to enhance coordination. They employ two complementary approaches: evolutionary dynamics and reinforcement learning experiments to analyze how these artificial agents interact with human agents in a mixed population setting.
AI.result: Artificial agents successfully shift learning dynamics to favor coordination outcomes. The study demonstrates that unilateral entry of artificial agents is feasible (adopters are not structurally penalized) and partial adoption can improve aggregate outcomes. However, in certain parameter regimes, non-adopters benefit disproportionately from the cooperation induced by adopters, creating an asymmetry that warrants strategic consideration.
AI.conclusion: While artificial agents can effectively coordinate outcomes and partial adoption provides benefits, the asymmetric payoff structure where non-adopters disproportionately benefit from adopter cooperation highlights important strategic considerations for AI deployment. This underscores that successful adoption of AI technology in multiagent settings requires careful consideration of these strategic dynamics rather than assuming uniform benefits across all population members.

title: Thousand-GPU Large-Scale Training and Optimization Recipe for AI-Native Cloud Embodied Intelligence Infrastructure
summary: Embodied intelligence is a key step towards Artificial General Intelligence (AGI), yet its development faces multiple challenges including data, frameworks, infrastructure, and evaluation systems. To address these issues, we have, for the first time in the industry, launched a cloud-based, thousand-GPU distributed training platform for embodied intelligence, built upon the widely adopted LeRobot framework, and have systematically overcome bottlenecks across the entire pipeline. At the data layer, we have restructured the data pipeline to optimize the flow of embodied training data. In terms of training, for the GR00T-N1.5 model, utilizing thousand-GPU clusters and data at the scale of hundreds of millions, the single-round training time has been reduced from 15 hours to just 22 minutes, achieving a 40-fold speedup. At the model layer, by combining variable-length FlashAttention and Data Packing, we have moved from sample redundancy to sequence integration, resulting in a 188% speed increase; π-0.5 attention optimization has accelerated training by 165%; and FP8 quantization has delivered a 140% speedup. On the infrastructure side, relying on high-performance storage, a 3.2T RDMA network, and a Ray-driven elastic AI data lake, we have achieved deep synergy among data, storage, communication, and computation. We have also built an end-to-end evaluation system, creating a closed loop from training to simulation to assessment. This framework has already been fully validated on thousand-GPU clusters, laying a crucial technical foundation for the development and application of next-generation autonomous intelligent robots, and is expected to accelerate the arrival of the era of human-machine integration.

AI.tldr: A cloud-based, thousand-GPU distributed training platform for embodied intelligence built upon LeRobot framework, achieving 40-fold training speedup through data pipeline restructuring, attention optimization, and infrastructure improvements.
AI.motivation: Embodied intelligence is a key step towards AGI but faces multiple challenges including data, frameworks, infrastructure, and evaluation systems that need to be addressed.
AI.method: A comprehensive solution including restructured data pipeline, thousand-GPU cluster training with FlashAttention, Data Packing, π-0.5 attention optimization, FP8 quantization, high-performance storage, 3.2T RDMA network, Ray-driven elastic AI data lake, and an end-to-end evaluation system.
AI.result: Single-round training time reduced from 15 hours to 22 minutes (40-fold speedup); variable-length FlashAttention + Data Packing: 188% speed increase; π-0.5 attention optimization: 165% speed increase; FP8 quantization: 140% speedup; framework validated on thousand-GPU clusters.
AI.conclusion: This framework lays a crucial technical foundation for the development and application of next-generation autonomous intelligent robots and is expected to accelerate the arrival of the era of human-machine integration.

title: The price of decentralization in managing engineering systems through multi-agent reinforcement learning
summary: Inspection and maintenance (I&M) planning involves sequential decision making under uncertainties and incomplete information, and can be modeled as a partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP). While single-agent deep reinforcement learning provides approximate solutions to POMDPs, it does not scale well in multi-component systems. Scalability can be achieved through multi-agent deep reinforcement learning (MADRL), which decentralizes decision-making across multiple agents, locally controlling individual components. However, this decentralization can induce cooperation pathologies that degrade the optimality of the learned policies. To examine these effects in I&M planning, we introduce a set of deteriorating systems in which redundancy is varied systematically. These benchmark environments are designed such that computation of centralized (near-)optimal policies remains tractable, enabling direct comparison of solution methods. We implement and benchmark a broad set of MADRL algorithms spanning fully centralized and decentralized training paradigms, from value-factorization to actor-critic methods. Our results show a clear effect of redundancy on coordination: MADRL algorithms achieve near-optimal performance in series-like settings, whereas increasing redundancy amplifies coordination challenges and can lead to optimality losses. Nonetheless, decentralized agents learn structured policies that consistently outperform optimized heuristic baselines, highlighting both the promise and current limitations of decentralized learning for scalable maintenance planning.

AI.tldr: Multi-agent deep reinforcement learning for scalable inspection and maintenance planning under varying system redundancy, showing coordination challenges increase with redundancy while decentralized policies still outperform heuristics.
AI.motivation: Single-agent deep reinforcement learning (DRL) struggles to scale to multi-component inspection and maintenance planning due to computational complexity. Multi-agent DRL (MADRL) offers scalability through decentralized decision-making, but coordination pathologies may degrade optimality.
AI.method: Authors create benchmark environments with systematically varied redundancy in deteriorating systems, implementing and benchmarking multiple MADRL algorithms spanning centralized training (value-factorization) and decentralized training (actor-critic), comparing against centralized (near-)optimal policies.
AI.result: MADRL achieves near-optimal performance in series-like configurations but suffers optimality losses as redundancy increases due to coordination challenges. Despite limitations, decentralized agents learn structured policies that consistently outperform optimized heuristic baselines.
AI.conclusion: Decentralized learning shows promise for scalable maintenance planning but faces fundamental coordination challenges in highly redundant systems. Future work should address these coordination pathologies while preserving scalability benefits.

title: ResWM: Residual-Action World Model for Visual RL
comment: Submit KDD2026
summary: Learning predictive world models from raw visual observations is a central challenge in reinforcement learning (RL), especially for robotics and continuous control. Conventional model-based RL frameworks directly condition future predictions on absolute actions, which makes optimization unstable: the optimal action distributions are task-dependent, unknown a priori, and often lead to oscillatory or inefficient control. To address this, we introduce the Residual-Action World Model (ResWM), a new framework that reformulates the control variable from absolute actions to residual actions -- incremental adjustments relative to the previous step. This design aligns with the inherent smoothness of real-world control, reduces the effective search space, and stabilizes long-horizon planning. To further strengthen the representation, we propose an Observation Difference Encoder that explicitly models the changes between adjacent frames, yielding compact latent dynamics that are naturally coupled with residual actions. ResWM is integrated into a Dreamer-style latent dynamics model with minimal modifications and no extra hyperparameters. Both imagination rollouts and policy optimization are conducted in the residual-action space, enabling smoother exploration, lower control variance, and more reliable planning. Empirical results on the DeepMind Control Suite demonstrate that ResWM achieves consistent improvements in sample efficiency, asymptotic returns, and control smoothness, significantly surpassing strong baselines such as Dreamer and TD-MPC. Beyond performance, ResWM produces more stable and energy-efficient action trajectories, a property critical for robotic systems deployed in real-world environments. These findings suggest that residual action modeling provides a simple yet powerful principle for bridging algorithmic advances in RL with the practical requirements of robotics.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Residual-Action World Model (ResWM), a novel RL framework that reformulates control from absolute actions to residual (incremental) actions, integrated into a Dreamer-style latent dynamics model with an Observation Difference Encoder for improved sample efficiency, asymptotic returns, and control smoothness on robotics tasks.
AI.motivation: Learning predictive world models from raw visual observations is challenging in RL, especially for robotics. Conventional model-based RL frameworks condition future predictions on absolute actions, which leads to optimization instability because optimal action distributions are task-dependent, unknown a priori, and often cause oscillatory or inefficient control.
AI.method: ResWM reformulates the control variable from absolute actions to residual actions (incremental adjustments relative to previous step), aligning with real-world control smoothness. The framework includes an Observation Difference Encoder that explicitly models changes between adjacent frames. ResWM is integrated into a Dreamer-style latent dynamics model with minimal modifications, with both imagination rollouts and policy optimization conducted in the residual-action space.
AI.result: Empirical results on DeepMind Control Suite demonstrate consistent improvements in sample efficiency, asymptotic returns, and control smoothness, significantly surpassing strong baselines like Dreamer and TD-MPC. ResWM produces more stable and energy-efficient action trajectories.
AI.conclusion: Residual action modeling provides a simple yet powerful principle for bridging algorithmic advances in RL with the practical requirements of robotics, offering stable long-horizon planning and energy-efficient control suitable for real-world robotic deployment.

title: CogSearch: A Cognitive-Aligned Multi-Agent Framework for Proactive Decision Support in E-Commerce Search
summary: Modern e-commerce search engines, largely rooted in passive retrieval-and-ranking models, frequently fail to support complex decision-making, leaving users overwhelmed by cognitive friction. In this paper, we introduce CogSearch, a novel cognitive-oriented multi-agent framework that reimagines e-commerce search as a proactive decision support system. By synergizing four specialized agents, CogSearch mimics human cognitive workflows: it decomposes intricate user intents, fuses heterogeneous knowledge across internal and external sources, and delivers highly actionable insights. Our offline benchmarks validate CogSearch's excellence in consultative and complex search scenarios. Extensive online A/B testing on JD.com demonstrates the system's transformative impact: it reduced decision costs by 5% and achieved a 0.41% increase in overall UCVR, with a remarkable 30% surge in conversion for decision-heavy queries. CogSearch represents a fundamental shift in information retrieval, moving beyond traditional relevance-centric paradigms toward a future of holistic, collaborative decision intelligence.
